name: Booker Ervin
abstract: Booker Telleferro Ervin II (October 31, 1930 – August 31, 1970) was an American tenor saxophone player. His tenor playing was characterised by a strong, tough sound and blues/gospel phrasing.

about_quotes.speaker: Sherrie Maricle
about_quotes.evidence: I accidentally twirled down to WSKG public radio in Binghamton. And I remember the song it was a Booker Ervin’s version of “The Lamp is Low.” And I heard that and I was like what is that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sherrie Maricle interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 18, 2001 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Ernie Fields
about_quotes.evidence: You had Booker Ervin in the band? ... Yeah. ... I got hold of Booker and he joined the band

about_quotes.speaker: John Heard
about_quotes.evidence: my first job was with Tommy Turrentine... and Booker Ervin was in the band
about_quotes.doc_title: John Heard interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Akiyoshi Toshiko
about_quotes.evidence: But one point … when I first had joined, there was Booker Ervin.
about_quotes.doc_title: Akiyoshi Toshiko — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: One of the memorable concerts there was one that was headed up by Teddy Charles, but included both Booker Ervin and Booker Little, both of whom had far too short careers, both of whom I did get to know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Avakian George
about_quotes.evidence: He got Clark Terry and Booker Ervin. I can’t remember who else. But the album is there, Joe Williams at Newport.
about_quotes.doc_title: Avakian George — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
